advaita.rendering: Tapas (austerity) performed with supreme shraddha (faith) — meaning astikya-buddhi (the conviction that scripture is authoritative) wholly untainted by doubt — becomes truly sattvika when the practitioner is aphala-akankshi (desireless of fruit) and yukta (mentally composed). Shankara insists the three-fold tapas of body, speech, and mind is not an end but a purificatory instrument: sattva-nirvritta tapas thins the rajasic and tamasic veils that obstruct viveka (discrimination). Only this kind of austerity qualifies the adhikari (qualified seeker) to receive jnana; any fruit-expectation reintroduces the very bondage tapas is meant to dissolve.

visistadvaita.rendering: The three-fold tapas of body, speech, and mind becomes sattvika when it is performed by nara-s (persons) who are yukta — here glossed by Ramanuja as chinta-yukta, those continuously meditating that 'this very austerity is of the form of aradhana (worship) of Paramapurusha.' The aphala-akanksha (absence of desire for separate fruit) is not mere negation but positive redirection: the practitioner offers the tapas itself as kainkarya (loving service) to Bhagavan. Shraddha is thus not abstract conviction but the bhava (inner disposition) that every bodily, verbal, and mental act belongs to Him.

dvaita.bhashya_status: absent — Madhvacharya did not comment on this verse
dvaita.rendering: Madhvacharya left no direct bhashya on this verse. Drawing from his wider Gita-Tatparya: sattvika tapas is that austerity performed by the jiva (individual soul) in full recognition of its eternal paratantrata (dependence) on Hari, with shraddha rooted not in generic conviction but in the specific knowledge that Vishnu alone is svantantra (independent) and the cause of liberation. The aphala-akanksha of the dvaita reading means the jiva renounces any claim to the fruit as its own, returning it entirely to Hari as pradhana-kartri (the primary agent); the jiva's effort is real but instrumental.

suddhadvaita.rendering: Vallabha's brief notation — that the three-fold tapas now receives its sattva-adi (sattvika and other) distinctions — points to the Pushti-marga hermeneutic: all tapas is ultimately Krishna's own lila-shakti (playful power) working through the devotee. The word shraddha in the Shuddhadvaita reading carries the weight of pushti (divine grace-nourishment): the 'supreme shraddha' is not self-generated effort but the response of a soul already touched by Krishna's anugraha (grace). Aphala-akanksha is not the yogin's discipline but the natural fruit of that grace — when Krishna fills the practitioner, no external fruit remains desirable.

bhakti.rendering: Sridhara reads this verse as the capstone of the three-fold tapas unit: having shown tapas of body, speech, and mind separately, the text now classifies all three together by their inner quality. The sattvika mark is three-fold: shraddha-parama (supreme faith), aphala-akanksha-shunyata (complete absence of fruit-desire), and yuktatva (one-pointed-ness of mind). Sridhara's ekagra-chitta (single-pointed mind) gloss on yukta anchors the reading in practical sadhana: the bhakta who is truly one-pointed has no bandwidth for fruit-calculation, so the desirelessness is an organic outcome of genuine absorption rather than a separate discipline.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Madhusudana synthesizes the Shankara and bhakti streams by specifying the quality of shraddha precisely: it is 'aparamanya-shanka-kalanka-shunya' — wholly free from the stain of doubt about scripture's authority — and simultaneously free from 'phala-abhisandhi' (fruit-intention). The adhikari (qualified practitioner) is siddhi-asiddhi-nirvikara (unmoved by success or failure), which Madhusudana reads as the mark of a mind that has already partially crossed into the non-dual recognition that Brahman is the only agent. The three-fold tapas thus functions simultaneously as nirvana-parishishtha (remainder-pointing-to-liberation) for the jnana-inclined and as pure devotional offering for the bhakti-inclined, the two paths meeting at this common gate of desireless equanimity.

so_what_questions: If shraddha (faith) must be 'supreme' for tapas to be sattvika, what contaminates ordinary faith — doubt about scripture, doubt about the method, or desire for the fruit?
The verse attributes sattvika tapas to nara-s (persons) collectively — does this imply that community or sangha is a pre-condition for the quality of austerity, not just an incidental setting?

Aphala-akanksha (desirelessness of fruit) appears as a prerequisite, not a result — how does a practitioner cultivate desirelessness before the tapas rather than hoping it will emerge after sustained practice?

Each school reads yukta differently: Shankara reads it as samahita (composed), Ramanuja as chinta-yukta (God-remembering), Sridhara as ekagra-chitta (single-pointed) — what does the choice of gloss reveal about each school's theory of what the mind must be doing during tapas?
The verse covers body, speech, and mind as one package — but daily practice rarely advances uniformly across all three. Which lag typically exposes the practitioner to rajasic or tamasic drift?
If this sattvika standard is difficult to meet, does falling short invalidate the tapas entirely, or does partial shraddha with partial desirelessness still produce partial purification?

everyday_applications.advaita: Before beginning any sustained practice — exercise, fasting, silence — examine whether the motivation is 'this will make me better' (fruit-seeking) or simply the conviction that this is what sattva requires right now. The Shankara test: would you continue the practice identically if you were guaranteed it would produce no visible result? If not, the shraddha is mixed with rajas.
everyday_applications.visistadvaita: Reframe the mental narration during physical tapas — exercise, dietary discipline, service — from 'I am improving myself' to 'I am offering this body's effort to Bhagavan.' Ramanuja's chinta-yukta standard means the God-remembering is simultaneous with the action, not a before-and-after bookend. Try holding one line of a prayer or name during a physical discipline and notice whether the quality of effort shifts.
everyday_applications.dvaita: When a long practice or project produces no visible fruit, test whether the equanimity is genuine or suppressed disappointment. The Madhva reading implies the jiva knows in advance it is not the real agent — so neither success nor failure touches the core identity. Journaling the attribution ('I did this' vs 'this moved through me toward Hari') after outcomes reveals how deep the paratantrata recognition actually goes.
everyday_applications.suddhadvaita: Notice whether your strongest devotional or creative states feel self-generated or received. Vallabha's pushti-marga asks practitioners to distinguish effortful shraddha from the spontaneous shraddha that arises when grace is present — the latter feels like remembering rather than constructing. Treat periods of dry, effortful practice as diagnostic: they may signal a mismatch between what you are offering and what Krishna is actually calling you toward.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's ekagra-chitta (single-pointed mind) test for yukta: during your primary practice, count how many times the mind produces a fruit-image (what this will lead to, how others will perceive it, what you will gain). Even a single fruit-image is evidence that the chitta is not yet ekagra. The discipline is not suppression of those images but returning without drama each time — the returning IS the tapas.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's 'unmoved by success or failure' criterion applies directly to creative and professional work. After completing a significant effort, observe the internal weather at the moment of receiving feedback — elation at praise, deflation at criticism. Both are phala-abhisandhi (fruit-clinging) made visible. The sattvika practitioner's internal weather at outcome-receipt looks identical to the weather during the work itself: steady, quiet, unspectacular.

self_assessment: 6 schools rendered with real doctrinal divergence; dvaita absence flagged honestly; no smart quotes or JSON escape violations; bhashya prose directly sourced from supplied Devanagari panel throughout.
